straight_beat.name: Walt Kieniewicz
straight_beat.column:
The Cubs lost again, but the box score tells a story of missed opportunities and stubborn resilience. Yesterday’s 13-7 defeat to the Phillies wasn’t just another loss—it was a snapshot of a team teetering between promise and peril. The offense, which looked lethal in the first half of the season, has sputtered in recent weeks, and the bullpen, once a source of stability, now feels like a revolving door of frustration.  

The Phillies’ 13 runs weren’t just the result of a few home runs. They were a product of relentless pressure, a lack of timely hitting, and a bullpen that couldn’t hold a lead. The Cubs’ starters, for all their early-season grit, have struggled to build momentum. Last night’s game was no different. The rotation’s latest victim, the Phillies’ starter, pitched efficiently, but the Cubs’ offense couldn’t capitalize. That’s what the box

optimist.name: Beatrice 'Bee' Vittorini
optimist.column:
The Cubs lost yesterday, 13-7, but let’s not let that sour the day. Wrigley Field’s ivy, ever the resilient green, clings to the walls despite the rain, and so do we. This team? They’re not just surviving—they’re thriving in the way that ivy does, creeping up, stubborn, and full of quiet determination.  

Let’s talk about Crow-Armstrong. That kid’s got a cannon for an arm, and yesterday, he reminded us why the Cubs drafted him in the first place. Gliding to that gap ball? That’s not just a play—it’s a statement. A reminder that even when the numbers don’t reflect it, there’s still magic in the way a pitcher commands his fastball, the way his legs move like a dancer,

pessimist.name: Tony Gedeski
pessimist.column:
The Cubs lost again. Not just lost, but collapsed in a way that makes you want to rip your hair out. You spend a billion on the marquee and forget to build a bullpen. Every Cubs summer is the same jazz and the same hangover.  

Last night’s 13-7 drubbing by the Phillies wasn’t just another loss—it was a textbook example of why we’re all stuck in this loop. The bullpen, that cursed appendage of the team, turned into a sieve. Kyle Hendricks, for all his promise, looked like a guy trying to pitch with a wet sock on. Javier Assad? He’s been a ghost since 2021. And let’s not forget the closer, who’s been a liability since the days of Aroldis Chapman. The front office keeps signing free agents like they’re buying lottery tickets, but when the chips are down, they’re still stuck with a bullpen that’s more like a punchline than a weapon.  

Ricketts gonna Ricketts. You think the ownership group learned anything from the 2016 World Series run? They didn’t. They built a palace in Wrigley Field, but the back end of the bullpen? Still a joke. It’s like they’re trying to impress the neighbors with a new porch light while the house is falling apart inside.  

The standings? 7-9, 3.0 games behind. It’s not just the math—it’s the feeling. You can’t help but wonder if this is the new normal. The Cubs are a team that’s always one step away from relevance, but never quite there. They’re like a guy who buys a new suit every year but still wears the same old tie. The marquee’s bright, but the rest of the outfit? A mess.  

And here’s the kicker: even when they’re winning,
